Metadata.Case_Number: Suit No 477 of 2012; Suit No 1015 of 2012; Registrar's Appeal No 246 of 2013; Registrar's Appeal No 247 of 2013; Summons No 4420 of 2013; Summons No 4419 of 2013; Summons No 2989 of 2013; Summons No 2990 of 2013

Party_Details.Name: Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd (AT) – Suit No 477 of 2012 (AT (Carolyn))

Facts.Text: Suit No 477 of 2012 is an action brought in the name of Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd against Linda Kao Chai-Chau, with conduct of the suit taken by shareholder Carolyn Fong as a derivative action.
In Suit No 477 of 2012, Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd is the company on whose behalf shareholder Carolyn Fong is pursuing a derivative action against former managing director Linda Kao Chai-Chau.

In Originating Summons No 505 of 2010, the High Court granted leave under section 216A of the Companies Act for shareholder Carolyn Fong to institute a derivative action on behalf of Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd against Linda Kao Chai-Chau.

Before 2011, shareholder Carolyn Fong applied for leave under section 216A of the Companies Act to commence a derivative action on behalf of Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd against managing director Linda Kao Chai-Chau.
By 2013, different law firms had been engaged to represent Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd in Suit No 477 of 2012 and Suit No 1015 of 2012.
Around 2006, Peter Fong transferred a majority of his shares in Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd to the Fong Foundation charitable trust, resulting in the Fong Foundation holding about 51% of the shares.
On 2008-04-25, Peter Fong, then Chairman and a significant shareholder of Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd, passed away.

At around the time of Peter Fong's death in 2008, he held approximately 3.6% of the shares in Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd, the Fong Foundation held about 51%, and the remaining shares were held by 11 other persons including Carolyn Fong with about 6.2%.

On 2013-09-02, parties attended a hearing before the High Court on Registrar's Appeals No 246 of 2013 and No 247 of 2013 concerning third party proceedings related to Suit No 477 of 2012 and Suit No 1015 of 2012.

On 2013-10-11, parties made further submissions before the High Court on questions concerning the legal consequences of a director following instructions of a controlling chairman in relation to Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd.

On 2013-11-04, an application by Carolyn Fong for leave to hand over conduct of the derivative action in Suit No 477 of 2012 to the Receivers and Managers of Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd was heard, with the Receivers and Managers taking a neutral position.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether, in Suit No 477 of 2012, losses to Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd from specified transactions can be allocated solely to the conduct of former managing director Linda Kao Chai-Chau or also to conduct attributable to Peter Fong such that contribution or indemnity between them is in issue.
Party_Details.Rule: Where multiple persons are each legally responsible in respect of the same damage suffered by a company, contribution between them may be available under statutory contribution regimes, and procedural rules allow a defendant to bring third party proceedings for contribution or indemnity connected with the original subject matter of the action.
Party_Details.Application: Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd, through the derivative action in Suit No 477 of 2012, seeks recovery for losses said to arise from specified transactions during the period when Linda Kao Chai-Chau was managing director and Peter Fong was Chairman and a significant shareholder. Linda Kao Chai-Chau seeks to involve the estate of Peter Fong in the same proceedings for potential contribution or indemnity in respect of any overlapping loss to Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd, using the third party procedure provided by the Rules of Court and the statutory contribution framework.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The judgment analyses whether illegality bars claims for contribution or indemnity, the statutory basis for contribution under sections 15, 16 and 19 of the Civil Law Act, and whether third party proceedings against the estate of Peter Fong are redundant given Linda Kao Chai-Chau’s defence. The court discusses authorities including Burrows v Rhodes and Jameson, K v P, Nganthavee, Yemen, Westcoast, and Panweld, and concludes that statutory contribution is not barred by illegality and that the third party notices are not redundant. The court reasons that there are possible scenarios in which Linda could be found liable to Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd while the estate of Peter Fong is also liable in respect of the same damage, justifying third party proceedings. The court allows Registrar's Appeals No 246 of 2013 and No 247 of 2013 and grants the applications to amend the third party notices, leaving questions of contribution apportionment and any discretionary exemption to the trial court after full evidence is heard.

Metadata.Case_Number: Suit No 1015 of 2012; Registrar's Appeal No 247 of 2013; Summons No 4419 of 2013; Summons No 2989 of 2013

Party_Details.Name: Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd (AT) – Suit No 1015 of 2012 (AT (RM))

Facts.Text: Suit No 1015 of 2012 is an action brought in the name of Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd by its Receivers and Managers against Linda Kao Chai-Chau and 15 other individuals, including corporate entities.
On 2012-01-17, Ernst & Young was appointed as Receivers and Managers of Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd pursuant to an application by shareholder Carolyn Fong.

On 2012-01-17, a consent order was made in connection with the appointment of Ernst & Young as Receivers and Managers of Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd, following an apparent agreement between Carolyn Fong and Linda Kao Chai-Chau.

After their appointment in January 2012, the Receivers and Managers of Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd reviewed company affairs and formed the view that there was evidence of diversions involving Linda Kao Chai-Chau and others that were detrimental to Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd, largely concerning events after Peter Fong's death.

Following their review, the Receivers and Managers commenced Suit No 1015 of 2012 on behalf of Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd against Linda Kao Chai-Chau and 15 other individuals in relation to transactions and events mainly occurring after Peter Fong's death.

On 2013-04-19, in Suit No 1015 of 2012, Linda Kao Chai-Chau commenced third party proceedings against the estate of Peter Fong seeking an indemnity in respect of Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd's claims.

On 2013-06-12, the Receivers and Managers of Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd applied by Summons No 2989 of 2013 to set aside the third party proceedings that Linda Kao Chai-Chau had commenced against the estate of Peter Fong in Suit No 1015 of 2012.
On 2013-07-19, the Assistant Registrar delivered an oral decision on applications concerning third party proceedings in Suit No 1015 of 2012 and Suit No 477 of 2012.

On 2013-08-26, an application was made to amend the third party notice in Suit No 477 of 2012 to add an alternative claim for contribution and to state that Peter Fong, as a director of Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd, owed the same duties as Linda Kao Chai-Chau.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether, in Suit No 1015 of 2012, losses to Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd from diversions of business after Peter Fong's death can be attributed solely to the conduct of Linda Kao Chai-Chau and other defendants, or also to conduct attributable to Peter Fong such that contribution or indemnity between them is in issue.
Party_Details.Rule: Where more than one person is legally responsible in respect of the same damage suffered by a company, statutory provisions on contribution allow one responsible person to seek contribution from another, and procedural rules permit third party proceedings to resolve related questions in the same action.
Party_Details.Application: In Suit No 1015 of 2012, the Receivers and Managers of Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd pursue claims concerning transactions mainly after Peter Fong's death. Linda Kao Chai-Chau seeks to involve the estate of Peter Fong through third party proceedings for potential contribution or indemnity in respect of any overlapping loss to Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd. The statutory contribution framework and third party procedure are used to align the resolution of Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd's claims with any allocation of responsibility between Linda Kao Chai-Chau and the estate of Peter Fong.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court considers whether the third party proceedings in Suit No 1015 of 2012 are redundant or unnecessary, given Linda Kao Chai-Chau’s defence that she acted on Peter Fong’s instructions. It examines whether there are scenarios where Linda could be held responsible to Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd while the estate of Peter Fong is also responsible for the same damage, justifying contribution. The court concludes that such scenarios are not fanciful and that the third party proceedings should proceed so that the trial court can determine, after hearing evidence, whether both Linda and Peter Fong’s estate are responsible in respect of the same damage and how any contribution should be apportioned. The court allows Registrar's Appeal No 247 of 2013 and the related application to amend the third party notice, leaving the exercise of the contribution discretion to the trial court.

Metadata.Case_Number: Suit No 477 of 2012; Suit No 1015 of 2012

Party_Details.Name: Kao Chai-Chau Linda

Facts.Text: In 1996, Linda Kao Chai-Chau was appointed as managing director of Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd by its majority shareholder and then Chairman, Peter Fong.
From 1996 up to 2012, Linda Kao Chai-Chau held the position of managing director of Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd.
The derivative action concerned alleged diversions of business opportunities from Chinese chemical companies, said to have taken place between 2000 and the date of the writ.
The derivative action also concerned alleged diversions from another Chinese company from around 2001.
The third claim related to acts occurring around 2002 and 2004.
Linda Kao Chai-Chau was the defendant in Suit No 477 of 2012 and the first defendant in Suit No 1015 of 2012.
Linda applied by Summons No 2990 of 2013 for leave to serve a third party notice against the Estate of PF seeking an indemnity, and later sought to amend the notice to add a claim for contribution.

On 2013-04-19, in Suit No 1015 of 2012, Linda Kao Chai-Chau commenced third party proceedings against the estate of Peter Fong seeking an indemnity in respect of claims brought by Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd.

On 2013-08-26, an application was made to amend the Notice (DA) so as to state that PF, as a director of AT, had owed the same fiduciary duties as Linda, and to add an alternative claim for contribution.

On 2013-09-02, Linda Kao Chai-Chau, through counsel, participated in a hearing of Registrar's Appeals No 246 of 2013 and No 247 of 2013 concerning her third party claims against the estate of Peter Fong.

On 2013-10-11, Linda Kao Chai-Chau, through counsel, made further submissions on legal questions relating to the effect of following instructions from a chairman who is said to be the controlling mind of a company.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether, in relation to transactions involving Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd between about 2000 and 2012, any financial loss to the company should be allocated solely to the conduct of managing director Linda Kao Chai-Chau or also to conduct attributable to Peter Fong such that Linda may seek contribution or indemnity from his estate.
Party_Details.Rule: A person who is or may be legally responsible in respect of damage suffered by another may, under statutory contribution provisions, seek contribution from any other person who is or may be legally responsible in respect of the same damage, and procedural rules allow a defendant to issue a third party notice to pursue such contribution or indemnity within the same proceedings.
Party_Details.Application: Linda Kao Chai-Chau faces claims in Suit No 477 of 2012 and Suit No 1015 of 2012 concerning transactions carried out while she was managing director of Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd. She seeks to use third party notices to involve the estate of Peter Fong so that, if both she and Peter Fong are found responsible in respect of the same loss to Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd, any allocation of that loss between them can be determined in the same proceedings under the statutory contribution framework.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The judgment records that Linda Kao Chai-Chau’s defence is that the impugned transactions were carried out in accordance with Peter Fong’s instructions or an understanding with him, and that she seeks indemnity or contribution from his estate in the event she is held liable. The court analyses whether public policy on illegality prevents such claims, concluding that statutory contribution under sections 15 and 16 of the Civil Law Act is not barred by illegality and that the ex turpi causa principle does not defeat a statutory contribution claim. The court further considers whether Linda’s third party claims are redundant because they overlap with her defence, and concludes that there are realistic scenarios in which Linda could be held liable to Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd while Peter Fong’s estate is also liable in respect of the same damage, justifying third party proceedings. The court allows Linda’s appeals and her applications to amend the third party notices, leaving the ultimate determination of liability and any contribution between Linda and the estate of Peter Fong to the trial court.

Metadata.Case_Number: Estate of Peter Fong – Third Party in Suit No 477 of 2012 and Suit No 1015 of 2012

Party_Details.Name: Estate of Peter Fong

Facts.Text: In 1996, Peter Fong was the majority shareholder and Chairman of Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd when Linda Kao Chai-Chau was appointed as managing director.

Around 2006, Peter Fong transferred the majority of his shares in Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd to the Fong Foundation charitable trust, after which he held about 3.6% of the shares and the Fong Foundation held about 51%.
On 2008-04-25, Peter Fong, then Chairman of Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd, passed away.

By 2013, the estate of Peter Fong was named as the intended third party in Suit No 477 of 2012 and as third party in Suit No 1015 of 2012 in relation to potential indemnity or contribution claims by Linda Kao Chai-Chau.

Up to the time of the appeals in 2013, the estate of Peter Fong did not file applications to set aside the third party notices or to object to the commencement of third party proceedings in Suit No 477 of 2012 or Suit No 1015 of 2012.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether the estate of Peter Fong may bear responsibility, alongside Linda Kao Chai-Chau, in respect of any loss suffered by Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd from the transactions in issue, such that contribution or indemnity between them is available.
Party_Details.Rule: If more than one person is legally responsible in respect of the same damage suffered by a company, statutory contribution provisions allow one such person to seek contribution from another, and third party procedures enable these questions to be determined within the same proceedings.
Party_Details.Application: The estate of Peter Fong is joined or intended to be joined as a third party in Suit No 477 of 2012 and Suit No 1015 of 2012 so that, if both Peter Fong and Linda Kao Chai-Chau are ultimately found responsible in respect of the same loss to Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd, the allocation of that loss between them can be addressed through contribution or indemnity within those suits.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The judgment notes that the estate of Peter Fong, though separately represented, did not participate in the applications to set aside or oppose the third party proceedings. The court considers whether, on the assumed facts, Peter Fong could be responsible in respect of the same damage as Linda Kao Chai-Chau, and concludes that this is a realistic possibility. The court reasons that if both the managing director and the Chairman caused loss to Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd through the transactions in question, statutory contribution between them may arise. The court therefore permits the third party proceedings against the estate of Peter Fong to continue and allows amendments to the third party notices, leaving the determination of any responsibility of the estate and any contribution to the trial court after full evidence is heard.
